articles.title: Mysuru braces for prolonged heatwave, no immediate rain relief in sight - The Hindu
articles.full_text:
Residents in Mysuru district will have to endure prevailing heatwave-like conditions for a few more days as no significant rainfall is expected in the near term, keeping temperatures above normal across the district.
The district is witnessing elevated summer temperatures due to seasonal factors and a continued lack of rainfall. The delay in pre-monsoon showers has worsened conditions, making the heat increasingly uncomfortable for people.
According to data from Karnataka State Natural Disaster Monitoring Centre, Mysuru city recorded a maximum temperature of 36.5°C on Tuesday (April 28). While the heatwave risk across all nine taluks remains categorised as “low” for the next seven days (until May 5), temperatures continue to hover at higher-than-usual levels.
Over the past week, temperatures have ranged between 35.9°C and 38.7°C, staying below the 39°C mark but still significantly above normal. Nanjangud and H.D. Kote recorded highs of 38.7°C and 38.6°C respectively on Monday (April 27).
Current observations indicate widespread above-normal heating across the district, with maximum temperatures fluctuating between 37°C and 40°C — notably higher than climatological averages.
As per guidelines issued by the India Meteorological Department, a heatwave is declared when temperatures are at least 4.5°C above normal or exceed 40°C in plains. Mysuru is now approaching, and in some pockets locally experiencing, such conditions, said a note on heatwave conditions issued here.
Officials note that rainfall remains deficient, contributing to the persistent rise in temperature. Key factors, as per the note, driving the heat include strong solar radiation due to clear skies, absence of cloud cover, delayed pre-monsoon showers, dry wind patterns over interior Karnataka, and broader climate variability.
Meanwhile, authorities have advised people in the district to avoid direct sun exposure between 12 noon and 3 p.m., stay well hydrated, wear light and loose cotton clothing, and take precautions against heat-related illnesses. A dedicated ward has been set up at K.R. Hospital and Cheluvamba Hospital, both attached to the Mysore Medical College and Research Institute (MMCRI), to treat heatstroke cases.
With rising temperatures and delayed pre-monsoon showers, heat stress is emerging as a growing concern. Anticipating an increase in such cases with prolonged heatwave, the MMCRI has put special arrangements in place to treat patients affected by extreme heat exposure. A 20-bed special ward has been established in the stone building of Krishna Rajendra Hospital, while separate 10-bed wards for women and children in Cheluvamba Hospital.
Published - April 29, 2026 07:42 pm IST
